perturbation.original_text: PageMaster Corporation shall be responsible for all fulfillment obligations of this promotion relating to paging services, including, but not limited to, timely delivery of pagers, paging services, defective goods handling, subcontracting, deadlines, and handling of consumer and regulatory inquiries and complaints.
perturbation.changed_text: PageMaster Corporation shall endeavor to fulfill obligations related to paging services, including, but not limited to, making efforts toward timely delivery of pagers, addressing defective goods, subcontracting considerations, deadlines, and attempting to handle consumer inquiries.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates PageMaster's responsibility for fulfillment. The modified text uses vague language like 'endeavor,' 'making efforts,' and 'attempting to handle,' which creates ambiguity and weakens PageMaster's obligation. Instead of shall it says endeavor.
perturbation.law_explanation: CA Bus. & Prof. Code § 17531 requires clear and conspicuous disclosures of all material conditions and limitations in advertisements, including those related to fulfillment. By using vague language instead of clear obligations, the modified text fails to ensure that all material conditions related to fulfillment are clearly disclosed, potentially misleading consumers and violating the law.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 2. Particular Offenses [17530 - 17539.6] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17531. It is unlawful for any person, firm, or corporation, in any newspaper, magazine, circular, form letter or any open publication, published, distributed, or circulated in this state, including over the Internet, or on any billboard, card, label, or other advertising medium, or by means of any other advertising device, to advertise, call attention to or give publicity to the sale of any merchandise, which merchandise is secondhand or used merchandise, or which merchandise is defective in any manner, or which merchandise consists of articles or units or parts known as “seconds,” or blemished merchandise, or which merchandise has been rejected by the manufacturer thereof as not first class, unless there is conspicuously displayed directly in connection with the name and description of that merchandise and each specified article, unit, or part thereof, a direct and unequivoca
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens PageMaster's obligations by using vague language like 'endeavor,' 'making efforts,' and 'attempting to handle,' instead of 'shall be responsible'. This creates ambiguity about their fulfillment obligations. CA Bus. & Prof. Code § 17531, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires clear disclosures of all material conditions. By using vague language, the modified text fails to ensure that all material conditions related to fulfillment are clearly disclosed. The original text mandates PageMaster's responsibility, while the modified text uses less direct language, creating ambiguity, and contradicting the legal requirement for clear and conspicuous disclosures.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge that preparation for and execution of the promotion necessitates the exchange of confidential and proprietary information relating and belonging to the parties to this Agreement, as well as to other third parties integral to the promotion, including, without limitation, the pager manufacturer and the airtime supplier (herein "Information"). Each party agrees (1) to review, examine, inspect, obtain or utilize the information only for the purpose of this promotion, (2) to otherwise hold such Information strictly confidential, (3) to prevent the disclosure of such Information to nonessential third parties without a "need to know", and (4) to insure that each party's employees, agents and representatives and those of any integral third party understand and are bound by the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that preparation for and execution of the promotion may involve the exchange of information deemed confidential or proprietary. Each party should consider limiting access to information only to individuals who seemingly require it and aim to maintain confidentiality to a reasonable extent.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear obligation to hold information strictly confidential. The modified text uses weaker language like 'may involve,' 'deemed confidential,' 'should consider,' 'seemingly require,' and 'aim to maintain confidentiality to a reasonable extent,' making the obligation ambiguous and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modification replaces strict confidentiality obligations with vague recommendations, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to protect trade secrets under California law. If one party does not protect confidential information adequately it may be subject to penalty.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the confidentiality obligations, potentially contradicting the requirement to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets as described in the Law Explanation, which references CUTSA (California Uniform Trade Secrets Act). The original text's clear obligation to hold information 'strictly confidential' is replaced with vaguer language like 'aim to maintain confidentiality to a reasonable extent.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly define 'reasonable efforts,' it does identify the relevant code (CIV 3426) as part of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, suggesting the importance of maintaining trade secret protection, which the modified text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Los Angeles County, California shall be the sole and exclusive venue for any litigation or dispute resolution relating to or arising out of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Los Angeles County, California will generally be the preferred location for any litigation or dispute resolution relating to or arising out of the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates Los Angeles County as the exclusive venue for disputes. The modified text replaces 'shall be' with 'will generally be the preferred location,' introducing ambiguity and suggesting that other venues might be possible, which contradicts the initial exclusivity.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly contradicting the statute, it renders the venue provision unenforceable. CCP § 395(a) details the general rules for venue in California, which can be altered by contract. However, the alteration must be clear and unambiguous. By making the venue preference 'general' rather than exclusive, the provision becomes subject to interpretation and challenge, thus failing to establish a definitive venue as intended under California law. Ambiguous venue provisions can lead to disputes over jurisdiction and forum, undermining the predictability and enforceability the original clause sought to establish.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. OF THE PLACE OF TRIAL, RECLASSIFICATION, AND COORDINATION OF CIVIL ACTIONS [392 - 404.9] ( Heading of Title 4 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. Place of Trial [392 - 403] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 7. ) 395. (a) Except as otherwise provided by law and subject to the power of the court to transfer actions or proceedings as provided in this title, the superior court in the county where the defendants or some of them reside at the commencement of the action is the proper court for the trial of the action. If the action is for injury to person or personal property or for death from wrongful act or negligence, the superior court in either the county where the injury occurs or the injury causing death occurs or the county where the defendants, or some of them reside at the commencement of the action, is a proper court for the trial of the action. In a proceeding for dissolution of marriage, the superior court in the county where either the petitioner or respondent has been a resident for three months next preceding the commencement of the proceeding is the proper court for the trial of the proceeding. In a proceeding for nullit
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates Los Angeles County as the 'sole and exclusive venue,' but the modified text introduces ambiguity by stating it 'will generally be the preferred location.' This change contradicts the intent of establishing a definitive venue, as now other venues might be possible. CCP 395(a) details the general rules for venue in California, which can be altered by contract. However, the alteration must be clear and unambiguous, and this change creates an ambiguous legal obligation, making it difficult to enforce. The introduction of ambiguity undermines the enforceability of the venue provision.
